pred_reason: The SWC-101 label is justified due to the use of Solidity 0.4.21, which is outdated and lacks modern overflow protections. Multiple arithmetic operations exist without sufficient safeguards, and the SafeMath implementation uses outdated assert statements. Evidence: Line 1: pragma solidity ^0.4.21 (outdated compiler version); SafeMath mul function uses assert (line 48); No overflow checks in token transfer operations (lines 118, 133)

findings.Description: The smart contract uses Solidity version 0.4.21, which is outdated and lacks modern security features and protections. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Increased risk of known compiler vulnerabilities, missing built-in security checks, and potential compatibility issues with modern tooling.

findings.Description: The ownership transfer mechanism in the Owned contract requires two separate transactions (changeOwner and acceptNewOwner), but there is no protection against front-running or accidental transfer to a wrong address. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential loss of contract ownership if newOwner is set incorrectly or if a malicious actor intercepts the acceptance transaction.
findings.Location: Owned contract: functions changeOwner and acceptNewOwner

findings.Description: The contract does not validate that addresses (e.g., in changeOwner, transfer, approve) are not the zero address. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Tokens could be burned or sent to an unrecoverable address, and ownership could be lost if transferred to address(0).
findings.Location: changeOwner function (line 30), transfer function (line 118), approve function (line 133), etc.

findings.Description: While SafeMath library is used, the mul function uses assert which consumes all gas on failure in older Solidity versions. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: In case of overflow, transactions will revert but consume all gas, which is less gas-efficient than modern revert patterns.

findings.Issue: No ERC20 Compliance Check for approve
findings.Description: The approve function does not follow the ERC20 standard which requires allowing changing approval from non-zero to non-zero (must first set to zero). This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential front-running vulnerability or incompatibility with some wallets/exchanges that expect ERC20 standard behavior.

findings.Description: The fallback function reverts all incoming Ether transactions. While this prevents accidental Ether sending, it may be intentional. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Any Ether sent to the contract will be lost, but prevents unintended Ether locking.
